museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: Christmas arrives in a fractured industry: Donkey Kong Jr. Math expands the Famicom catalogue, UK micros become gift machines, and the crash is now part of the atmosphere rather than a distant warning.
museum.curatorNote: December is framed as a seasonal exhibit. The strongest exact software anchor here is Donkey Kong Jr. Math on Famicom; many UK entries are late-1983 or Christmas-shelf context and are marked that way.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: December shows the medium surviving by changing rooms.

items.title: Donkey Kong Jr. Math releases for Famicom
items.body: Nintendo's early Famicom catalogue gained an educational oddity, a reminder that the Japanese console story was still young and experimental.
items.sourceNote: Donkey Kong Jr. Math references list December 12, 1983 in Japan.

items.title: Famicom hardware issues shadow early success
items.body: Nintendo's young console had early technical problems that required attention, even as its long-term future was only beginning to form.
items.sourceNote: Famicom histories describe 1983 hardware issues/recall context.

items.title: UK microcomputers dominate the gift conversation
items.body: Spectrum, C64, BBC Micro, Electron and Dragon all offered a way to buy games while claiming education or future skills.

items.title: The North American console crash is fully visible
items.body: The year's collapse had become part of how games were discussed, even if players elsewhere were discovering a different route through computers.
items.sourceNote: Crash histories describe 1983 collapse and publisher failures.

items.title: Late-year UK software gives the year a local afterglow
items.body: Manic Miner, Jetpac, Atic Atac, Ant Attack and other Spectrum titles made 1983 feel like a beginning in Britain, not only an ending.

items.title: Donkey Kong Jr. Math
items.body: An early educational Famicom cartridge, strange now but revealing of Nintendo's first home-console experiments.

items.title: Atic Atac
items.body: A late-1983 Ultimate release that turned rooms, keys and creatures into a fast maze of memory.

items.title: Ant Attack
items.body: A memorable isometric survival game for the Spectrum, often framed as a landmark in 3D presentation.

items.title: Manic Miner
items.body: A Christmas-list game for a machine now rich with identity.

items.title: Jetpac
items.body: Still a benchmark for compact, fast British micro design.

items.title: Spy Hunter
items.body: A stylish late-year cabinet that made driving feel dangerous and cinematic.

items.title: Track & Field
items.body: A noisy competitive cabinet built for spectators and sore fingers.

items.title: Famicom launch library
items.body: Donkey Kong, Popeye and early additions formed the small but important Japanese catalogue.
items.sourceNote: Famicom launch and early software context.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: December's machines were presents, gambles and futures.

items.title: ZX Spectrum 48K
items.body: A strong UK Christmas machine because the software shelf was already lively and comparatively cheap.

items.title: Commodore 64
items.body: A more expensive but powerful games computer, helped by its sound and graphics reputation.

items.title: Acorn Electron
items.body: A desirable British micro with a serious pedigree, but supply issues complicated its first Christmas.

items.title: Famicom
items.body: Still Japan-only in this story, still early, and already part of a different answer to 1983's console crisis.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: December's covers became buying guides and memory objects.

items.title: Electronic Games
items.headline: Year-end games after the crash
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games cover for December 1983.
items.curatorNote: A US videogame magazine at year-end had to balance play with industry damage.
items.body: A US videogame magazine at year-end had to balance play with industry damage.

items.title: Computer and Video Games
items.headline: Christmas games for arcades and micros
items.curatorNote: C&VG was exactly the kind of shelf object a UK buyer needed before choosing tapes and machines.
items.body: C&VG was exactly the kind of shelf object a UK buyer needed before choosing tapes and machines.

items.title: Your Computer
items.headline: The computer under the tree
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Your Computer cover for December 1983.
items.curatorNote: The serious-computer cover helped justify a very games-heavy Christmas present.
items.body: The serious-computer cover helped justify a very games-heavy Christmas present.

items.title: ZX Computing
items.headline: Spectrum software becomes a culture
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed ZX Computing cover for winter 1983.
items.curatorNote: By winter, the Spectrum did not need to prove games existed for it. It needed readers to choose between them.
items.body: By winter, the Spectrum did not need to prove games existed for it. It needed readers to choose between them.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: December's network was family, paper and patience.

items.title: Gift guides were navigation
items.body: The right magazine could decide which machine seemed sensible.

items.title: Cassette culture felt communal
items.body: Loading screens, copied notes and shared frustrations became part of ownership.

items.title: Japan's console story was still remote
items.body: The Famicom was changing history without yet changing most UK living rooms.

items.title: Crash stories simplified fast
items.body: By year-end, messy market causes were already becoming memorable symbols.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: December 1983 felt like an ending only if you looked from one angle.

items.title: The console shelf looked wounded
items.body: In North America especially, 1983 had damaged trust.

items.title: The UK bedroom felt newly powerful
items.body: A micro and a stack of tapes could make the future feel personal.

items.title: Nintendo's future was still hidden nearby
items.body: The Famicom had launched, but its global meaning had not yet unfolded.

items.title: Games survived by changing containers
items.body: Cartridge, cassette, disk, cabinet: the medium moved where it could breathe.
